content.text: Comprehensions are one of Python's most expressive features: they let you build lists, dicts, and sets from any iterable in a single, readable line. In agent pipelines they appear constantly — filtering tool call results, normalizing API responses, building prompt batches, and deduplicating retrieved document sources. Mastering comprehensions means you can replace five-line loops with one-line intent.
is a concise expression that iterates over an iterable, optionally filters elements with an
clause, applies an expression to each surviving element, and collects the results into a new collection. Python provides four forms:
[expr for x in xs if cond]
— produces an ordered list, preserving iteration order.
{k: v for x in xs if cond}
{expr for x in xs if cond}
— produces an unordered set, automatically deduplicating values.
(expr for x in xs if cond)
— lazy; yields values one at a time without materializing a full collection.
All four share the same clause order: expression first, then
. This ordering matches natural language: "give me

Agent code lives on data transformation. Every time the agent calls a tool it gets back a list of results. Every time it retrieves documents it gets a ranked batch. Every time it formats a prompt it maps message objects to strings. These patterns repeat hundreds of times in any non-trivial pipeline, and comprehensions express each one without ceremony: no temporary variable, no explicit
, no closing brace on its own line.
Beyond aesthetics, CPython optimizes the bytecode for comprehensions differently from generic
loops. A list comprehension compiles to a
opcode inside its own implicit function scope, avoiding repeated attribute lookup on
. This means comprehensions are measurably faster for medium-sized collections — not the bottleneck in an LLM call, but meaningful when pre-processing thousands of document chunks before embedding.
— the map-filter pattern compresses from four to five lines down to one, reducing visual noise and making the intent obvious at a glance.
clause structure mirrors English prose, which lowers cognitive load when reading unfamiliar code.
bytecodes in comprehension scopes avoid per-iteration attribute lookup overhead compared to manual append loops.
— a comprehension always returns a fresh collection; it never mutates the source iterable, eliminating a class of aliasing bugs.
— comprehensions nest inside function arguments, other comprehensions, and f-strings without needing a named intermediate variable.
— loop variables inside a comprehension do not leak into the enclosing scope (unlike a bare
loop), which prevents subtle variable shadowing bugs.
— once a comprehension requires two or more nested
clauses with non-trivial expressions, it becomes harder to read than equivalent named loops.
— comprehensions are meant to be pure transforms. Mixing in I/O (
, file writes, API calls) inside a comprehension hides effects and is widely considered a code smell.
— list/dict/set comprehensions build the entire output in memory immediately. For millions of items this may be undesirable; generator expressions solve this.
— a comprehension always processes the full iterable (modulo the
— if an element raises inside the expression or filter, the traceback points at the comprehension line, not the specific element. A loop with a named variable is easier to debug in that case.
Python evaluates a comprehension strictly left-to-right: (1) evaluate the outermost iterable expression, (2) bind the loop variable for each element, (3) evaluate the optional
guard — skip the element if falsy, (4) evaluate the output expression, (5) append to the result container. All four steps run in a private implicit scope so the loop variable never leaks.

# Basic: [expression  for item  in iterable  if condition]
tool_names = [call["name"] for call in tool_calls]

# With filter
high_scores = [doc["text"] for doc in docs if doc["score"] >= 0.8]

# Nested for — cartesian product
pairs = [(model, task) for model in models for task in tasks]
# Order: outer 'models' loop is leftmost; same as:
# for model in models:
#     for task in tasks:
#         pairs.append((model, task))

# Conditional expression (ternary) — different from filter if
labels = ["retry" if status > 500 else "ok" for status in response_codes]

# (expression  for item  in iterable  if condition)
# Does NOT build a list — yields values lazily
total_tokens = sum(msg["tokens"] for msg in conversation)

# Pass directly to functions that accept iterables
any_failed  = any(r["status"] >= 500 for r in responses)
all_success = all(r["status"] < 400 for r in responses)

# Only materialize when needed
first_hit = next((doc for doc in docs if doc["score"] > 0.9), None)

The diagram below shows how each clause of a comprehension maps to a processing stage. Every element from the input iterable flows through the optional filter gate and then through the expression transform before landing in the output collection.
when the transformation is a single pure expression per element — no branching, no I/O, no mutation.
when you want a fresh collection, not an in-place mutation of an existing one.
to build lookup tables from API response arrays — e.g.,
for O(1) access by ID later.
to deduplicate retrieved document IDs before passing them to a re-ranker or database query.
— these consume elements one at a time, so materializing a list first wastes memory.
targets, logging per element, or more than one level of nesting.
when the iterable has millions of elements and the full result is never needed at once — a generator or streaming pipeline is better.
The implementation checklist for any comprehension:
Decide the output type: list, dict, set, or generator.
Write the expression — the value that goes into the output — first.
guard to filter elements before the expression is evaluated.
If the expression is longer than roughly 60 characters, extract it into a named helper function and call that instead.

# Building an agent message batch from raw API records
raw_records = [
    {"role": "user",      "content": "What is the weather?", "ts": 1700000001},
    {"role": "assistant", "content": "",                      "ts": 1700000002},  # empty
    {"role": "user",      "content": "  stock price  ",      "ts": 1700000003},
    {"role": "tool",      "content": "72F and sunny",        "ts": 1700000004},
]

# Step 1: extract only user messages with non-empty, stripped content
user_messages = [
    r["content"].strip()
    for r in raw_records
    if r["role"] == "user" and r["content"].strip()
]
# ["What is the weather?", "stock price"]

# Step 2: build prompt lines
prompt_lines = [f"- {msg}" for msg in user_messages]
# ["- What is the weather?", "- stock price"]

# Step 3: build a lookup by role for the latest content of each role
latest_by_role = {r["role"]: r["content"] for r in raw_records}
# {"user": "  stock price  ", "assistant": "", "tool": "72F and sunny"}
Patterns that appear frequently in agent code:

# --- Filtering ---
# Keep only tool calls that succeeded
successful = [tc for tc in tool_calls if tc["status"] == "success"]

# Keep only documents above a relevance threshold
relevant_docs = [d for d in retrieved if d["score"] >= 0.75]

# --- Transformation ---
# Extract all tool names from a list of tool call objects
names = [tc["name"] for tc in tool_calls]

# Truncate long document texts to 512 characters for a prompt
snippets = [doc["text"][:512] for doc in docs]

# Build numbered prompt lines
numbered = [f"{i+1}. {doc['text'][:200]}" for i, doc in enumerate(docs)]

# --- Dict building ---
# Index documents by their ID for O(1) lookup
doc_index = {doc["id"]: doc for doc in docs}

# Map tool name -> first matching call
first_call = {tc["name"]: tc for tc in reversed(tool_calls)}

# --- Set deduplication ---
# Collect unique source URLs from retrieved chunks
source_urls = {chunk["source_url"] for chunk in chunks if chunk.get("source_url")}

# --- Generator expressions ---
# Total token count without building an intermediate list
total = sum(msg.get("tokens", 0) for msg in conversation)

# Find first document above threshold without scanning all
first = next((d for d in docs if d["score"] > 0.95), None)

# Conditional expression inside (ternary — all items kept, value changes)
statuses = ["high" if d["score"] > 0.8 else "low" for d in docs]

Consider an agent that calls a web-search tool and receives a batch of results. Before passing them to the language model as context, it needs to: (1) strip whitespace from snippets, (2) drop results with no snippet, (3) deduplicate by URL, and (4) format them as numbered lines.

from typing import TypedDict

class SearchResult(TypedDict):
    url: str
    title: str
    snippet: str

def prepare_context(results: list[SearchResult], max_results: int = 8) -> str:
    """
    Filter, deduplicate, and format web-search results into a context block
    suitable for insertion into a system prompt.
    """
    # 1. Clean snippets and drop empties
    cleaned = [
        {**r, "snippet": r["snippet"].strip()}
        for r in results
        if r["snippet"].strip()
    ]

    # 2. Deduplicate by URL, preserving the first occurrence (highest rank)
    seen: set[str] = set()
    unique = []
    for r in cleaned:
        if r["url"] not in seen:
            seen.add(r["url"])
            unique.append(r)

    # 3. Slice to max_results
    top = unique[:max_results]

    # 4. Format as numbered context lines
    lines = [
        f"{i+1}. [{r['title']}]({r['url']}): {r['snippet']}"
        for i, r in enumerate(top)
    ]

    return "
".join(lines)


# Example usage
sample_results: list[SearchResult] = [
    {"url": "https://example.com/a", "title": "Alpha",  "snippet": "  first result  "},
    {"url": "https://example.com/b", "title": "Beta",   "snippet": ""},
    {"url": "https://example.com/a", "title": "Alpha2", "snippet": "duplicate url"},
    {"url": "https://example.com/c", "title": "Gamma",  "snippet": "third result"},
]

context_block = prepare_context(sample_results)
print(context_block)
# 1. [Alpha](https://example.com/a): first result
# 2. [Gamma](https://example.com/c): third result
hides I/O, runs regardless of whether the result is used, and is indistinguishable from a pure transform at a glance. Use a
loop when side effects are intended.
[x for x in items if cond]
[x if cond else y for x in items]
is always an empty dict, not an empty set — use
— two levels of nesting are valid but a third level is almost always a sign the logic should be extracted into a named helper function or broken into sequential steps.
— because comprehensions create their own scope, the loop variable does
shadow an outer variable with the same name in Python 3. Still, reusing a name like
across multiple comprehensions in the same function increases confusion; use descriptive names.
[f(x) for x in items if f(x) > 0]
twice per element. Cache the result with a walrus operator:
[y for x in items if (y := f(x)) > 0]
Keep the expression short enough to read on one line without horizontal scrolling. If it exceeds about 80 characters, define a helper and call it:
Assign the result to a well-named variable. An unnamed comprehension at statement level (not assigned) is always wrong.
Prefer generator expressions over list comprehensions when the result is passed directly to
, or any function that iterates lazily.
Avoid more than one level of nesting. Two
clauses can be acceptable for cartesian products; three is almost always a maintenance problem.
When you need to filter and transform with the same expensive computation, use the walrus operator
(Python 3.8+) to avoid calling the function twice.
Do not modify the source iterable inside the comprehension expression or filter.
Use type annotations on the result variable when the element type is non-obvious:
names: list[str] = [r['name'] for r in results]

# Walrus operator: compute once, filter and use the same value
from anthropic import Anthropic

client = Anthropic()

def score_chunks(chunks: list[dict]) -> list[dict]:
    """Return only chunks whose embedding cosine similarity exceeds 0.75."""
    # Without walrus — computes embedding twice:
    # [c for c in chunks if embed(c["text"]) > 0.75]  # BUG: embed called twice
    #
    # Correct with walrus — embed called once per chunk:
    return [
        {"chunk": c, "score": s}
        for c in chunks
        if (s := cosine_similarity(c["embedding"], query_embedding)) > 0.75
    ]

def cosine_similarity(a: list[float], b: list[float]) -> float:
    dot = sum(x * y for x, y in zip(a, b))
    norm_a = sum(x * x for x in a) ** 0.5
    norm_b = sum(x * x for x in b) ** 0.5
    return dot / (norm_a * norm_b) if norm_a and norm_b else 0.0

# Type-annotated comprehension
query_embedding: list[float] = [0.1, 0.2, 0.3]
chunks: list[dict] = [
    {"text": "Paris is the capital of France.", "embedding": [0.15, 0.22, 0.29]},
    {"text": "The sky is blue.",                "embedding": [0.8,  0.1,  0.0]},
]
ranked: list[dict] = score_chunks(chunks)
In CPython, a list comprehension compiles to a
followed by a loop that uses the
opcode. This is faster than a
because the bytecode avoids loading the
attribute from the list object on every iteration.

However, the performance gap is generally small (20–40 percent) and completely invisible when the body involves a network call, a LLM API round-trip, or even a regex match. Focus on correctness and readability first; benchmark only when profiling confirms a comprehension is the bottleneck.

For large-scale agent systems — pipelines over millions of document chunks — the choice between a list comprehension and a generator expression matters more than the comprehension-vs-loop speed difference:
: materializes the entire output in RAM immediately. Suitable when you need random access, multiple passes, or need to know
: yields elements lazily; only one element lives in RAM at a time. Suitable when feeding a streaming API, writing to a file, or passing to
compose generator-based pipelines without materializing intermediate results.

import itertools
from typing import Iterator

def stream_relevant_chunks(
    batches: list[list[dict]],
    threshold: float = 0.7,
) -> Iterator[dict]:
    """
    Yield chunks above threshold from multiple batches without
    holding all batches in memory simultaneously.
    """
    # itertools.chain flattens batches lazily
    all_chunks = itertools.chain.from_iterable(batches)
    # Generator expression — filters lazily
    return (chunk for chunk in all_chunks if chunk["score"] >= threshold)


batches = [
    [{"text": "doc1", "score": 0.9}, {"text": "doc2", "score": 0.5}],
    [{"text": "doc3", "score": 0.8}, {"text": "doc4", "score": 0.3}],
]

# Consume lazily — no intermediate list
for chunk in stream_relevant_chunks(batches, threshold=0.75):
    print(chunk["text"])
# doc1
# doc3
— type checkers infer the element type of comprehensions from the iterable's element type. Annotate the result variable (
(unnecessary list comprehension that can be replaced by
, where a generator expression suffices). It also flags
(function definition in loop without closing over the variable).
(consider using a generator instead of a list comprehension).
import dis; dis.dis('[x*2 for x in range(5)]')
opcode and the implicit inner function frame, useful for understanding scoping behavior.
python3 -m timeit '[x*2 for x in range(1000)]'
vs. the loop equivalent lets you measure the speed difference empirically.
— standard library module that extends generator-style pipelines.
cover patterns that comprehensions cannot express in a single expression.
— for fold/accumulate patterns that require carrying state across elements,
is more explicit than a comprehension with a mutable accumulator captured in a closure.
Four common alternatives exist; each has a distinct niche:
[expr for x in xs if c]
loop when the loop body involves
, multiple appends to different lists, or logging per element. The
builtins are lazy in Python 3 but less readable than comprehensions for most Python developers; prefer comprehensions unless interoperating with code that already uses
for pipelines that must remain lazy through multiple stages.
Comprehensions are pure transforms and introduce no unique security surface of their own. However several reliability patterns are worth knowing:
— if a dict key is absent,
raises inside the comprehension with only a line number in the traceback, not the offending element. Defensive options:
[x for x in items if x is not None]
creates a separate list for each key (correct). Do not use
as a shortcut — it shares the same list object across all keys.
— if the source iterable can grow unboundedly (streaming results, large files), switch to a generator to prevent OOM. Add a
, but note the whole list is built before slicing;
guard does not sanitize input, it only filters. Always validate and sanitize untrusted data before storing it, regardless of whether a comprehension or loop is used.
— set comprehensions do not guarantee insertion order. If order matters for prompt construction or deterministic output, use a list comprehension or sort the set.
expression for var in iterable if condition
at the end is a filter; a ternary
is a value transform — they are different and both valid but easy to confuse.
Comprehensions create their own scope; loop variables do not leak in Python 3.
to avoid calling expensive functions twice — once in the filter and once in the expression.
Do not put I/O, mutations, or side effects inside comprehensions; use a
Keep complexity low: no more than two
clauses, no nested comprehensions as the expression.
In agentic code, dict comprehensions are the fastest way to index API results by ID; set comprehensions are the fastest way to deduplicate document sources.
. Raw items enter from the left. An optional quality inspector (the

guard) waves off rejects without stopping the belt. Each item that passes is reshaped by the stamping press (the expression). Finished items drop into the output bin (list, dict, set, or generator). One pass, one new container, no modification of the incoming supply.
If you can say the comprehension aloud in that pattern and it sounds clear, it belongs as a comprehension. If you have to pause to figure out the structure, it belongs as a loop.
